name: Example Mapping
description: A collaborative discovery technique that breaks a user story into its business rules and concrete examples. Using four colours of card (story, rules, examples, questions), the team explores a story together before development, surfacing unknowns early and agreeing scope.
why_it_exists: Are we agreed on what this story means before we build it?
what_good_looks_like: Each rule has at least one concrete example; Open questions are captured rather than guessed; The whole story fits on one wall in 25 minutes
common_pitfalls: Turning it into a solution design session; Skipping the questions card and assuming agreement; Running it without a tester or business voice present
use_this_when: Refining a story before a sprint; A story feels ambiguous or large; Three Amigos conversations
Generic Use Case - When: During backlog refinement, before a story enters a sprint
Generic Use Case - What: The team maps rules and examples for a candidate story
Generic Use Case - Summary: A short, structured conversation that turns a vague story into agreed rules, worked examples and a list of open questions.

name: Dot Voting
description: A simple group decision technique where participants allocate a fixed number of dots across options to reveal collective preference. It quickly narrows a long list to a shortlist without lengthy debate.
why_it_exists: Which options does the group care about most?
what_good_looks_like: Everyone votes at the same time to avoid anchoring; The criteria for voting are clear and shared; Results spark a focused conversation, not a final verdict
common_pitfalls: Treating the result as a binding decision; Letting people vote sequentially and follow the crowd; Too many dots per person, which flattens the signal
use_this_when: Prioritising ideas after a brainstorm; Narrowing retrospective topics; Choosing between options in a workshop
Generic Use Case - When: At the end of a divergent ideation activity
Generic Use Case - What: Participants place dots against the options they value most
Generic Use Case - Summary: A fast, visual way to surface group preference and move from a long list to a focused shortlist.

name: Affinity Mapping
description: A sense-making technique for organising a large number of ideas, observations or research notes into natural groupings. Participants cluster related items, then name each cluster, revealing themes that were not obvious in the raw data.
why_it_exists: What themes emerge from everything we have gathered?
what_good_looks_like: Clusters are named by the group, not pre-defined; Outliers are kept visible rather than forced into a group; Themes lead to a clear next action
common_pitfalls: Imposing categories before clustering; One person driving the grouping; Stopping at clusters without naming the insight
use_this_when: Synthesising user research; Making sense of retrospective input; Grouping a large brainstorm
Generic Use Case - When: After gathering many notes from research or a workshop
Generic Use Case - What: The group clusters related notes and names the themes
Generic Use Case - Summary: A structured way to turn a wall of individual notes into a small set of named themes the team can act on.
